title: Compound Interest Calculator — Watch Your Money Grow
description: Calculate compound interest over time with monthly contributions. See how your investment grows year by year with the power of compounding.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is the difference between simple interest and compound interest?
faq.a1: Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal, so it grows linearly — the same dollar amount each year. Compound interest is calculated on the principal plus all previously earned interest, producing exponential growth. At 7% annual rate, $10,000 simple interest earns $700/year always. Compounded monthly, it earns $724 in year 1, $1,379 in year 10, and over $7,700 in year 30 — the same rate, dramatically different outcomes.
faq.q2: How does compounding frequency affect the final amount?
faq.a2: More frequent compounding results in higher returns. For $10,000 at 5% over 10 years: annually = $16,288.95, quarterly = $16,453.09, monthly = $16,470.09, daily = $16,474.61. The gap is modest over 10 years but grows meaningfully over longer periods. Practically, monthly compounding (common for savings accounts) is nearly equivalent to daily compounding.
faq.a3: The Rule of 72 estimates how many years it takes to double your money: divide 72 by your annual interest rate. At 6%, money doubles in 12 years. At 8%, in 9 years. At 4%, in 18 years. This rule illustrates why even a 2% difference in annual return produces radically different long-term wealth — and why fees that reduce your effective return by 1% are so costly.
faq.q4: What does 'Total Contributions' mean in the output?
faq.a4: Total Contributions is the real money you deposited — initial principal plus all monthly payments. For $5,000 initial and $200/month over 20 years: total contributions = $5,000 + ($200 × 240) = $53,000. If the final amount is $115,000, the $62,000 difference is pure compound interest — money the investment created without additional deposits.
faq.q5: Can I use this for savings accounts, bonds, or stock market projections?
faq.a5: Yes for all three. Savings accounts and CDs use guaranteed fixed rates — the calculator applies directly. For bonds, use the coupon rate. For stock market projections, use historical average returns (e.g., 7% inflation-adjusted for S&P 500 over long periods) as estimates — actual annual returns vary significantly, but long-run averages are a reasonable planning assumption.
faq.q6: How much does starting 10 years earlier affect the final result?
faq.a6: $200/month at 7%: starting at age 25 accumulates approximately $525,000 by age 65. Starting at 35 accumulates approximately $243,000 — less than half, despite investing only 10 fewer years. Those first 10 years of compounding create more wealth than the following 30 years of contributions combined. This is the most powerful argument for starting early regardless of how small the initial investment.
faq.q7: Does compound interest account for inflation?
faq.a7: No — this calculator shows nominal (pre-inflation) returns. In real purchasing-power terms, gains are reduced by the annual inflation rate. To get inflation-adjusted projections, subtract expected inflation from your interest rate input (e.g., enter 4% instead of 7% if you expect 3% inflation). For planning horizons of 20+ years, always think in real returns, not nominal.
faq.q8: What is the difference between APY and APR?
faq.a8: APR (Annual Percentage Rate) is the stated annual rate before compounding effects. APY (Annual Percentage Yield) is the actual annual return after compounding is applied — it is always equal to or higher than APR. An APR of 5% compounded monthly gives an APY of 5.116%. For investments and savings, APY is the number that matters for comparing offers.

content.whatIs:
Bileşik faiz hesaplayıcı, faizi yalnızca başlangıç anaparasına değil, önceden birikmiş tüm faize uygulayarak bir yatırımın gelecekteki değerini tahmin eder. Bu kendini güçlendiren mekanizma — faizin faiz kazanması — Albert Einstein'ın 'dünyanın sekizinci harikası' olarak adlandırdığı şeydir. Matematiksel gerçeklik inkâr edilemez: bileşik faiz, sıradan insanlar için mevcut en güçlü uzun vadeli servet yaratma gücüdür.

Doğrusal büyüyen basit faizin aksine, bileşik faiz üstel olarak büyür. Aylık bileşik %7 yıllık getiriyle 10.000 ₺'lik yatırım, 30 yılda 81.000 ₺'yi aşar — 10.000 ₺'lik bir tabandan 71.000 ₺'den fazla faiz kazanılır. Aylık 200 ₺ katkı eklendiğinde, 30 yıllık toplam yaklaşık 243.000 ₺'ye ulaşır; bunun 171.000 ₺'si saf bileşiklemeden, yalnızca 72.000 ₺'si gerçek mevduatlardan gelir.

Bu hesaplayıcı tüm standart bileşikleme sıklıklarını (yıllık, üç aylık, aylık, günlük) ve isteğe bağlı düzenli aylık katkıları destekler. Emeklilik tasarruflarını planlamak, üniversite fonlarını modellemek, mevduat veya tasarruf hesabı tekliflerini değerlendirmek veya sabit veya varsayılan bir getiri oranıyla herhangi bir yatırımın uzun vadeli büyümesini tahmin etmek için kullanın.
content.formula:
Temel Bileşik Faiz Formülü:
A = P × (1 + r/n)^(n×t)

Burada:
  A = nihai tutar
  P = anapara (başlangıç yatırımı)
  r = ondalık olarak yıllık faiz oranı (ör. %7 için 0,07)
  n = yıl başına bileşikleme dönemi sayısı (1=yıllık, 4=üç aylık, 12=aylık, 365=günlük)
  t = yıl cinsinden süre

Aylık Katkılarla (Anüite Gelecek Değeri):
FV = PMT × [((1 + r/n)^(n×t) − 1) / (r/n)]
Toplam = A + FV

Toplam Faiz = Nihai Tutar − (P + PMT × 12 × t)
content.example:
Örnek 1 — Tek seferlik yatırım:
10.000 ₺, %7 aylık bileşik, 30 yıl, katkı yok.
A = 10.000 × (1,005833)^360 ≈ 81.397 ₺. Kazanılan faiz: 71.397 ₺.

Örnek 2 — Düzenli katkılarla:
5.000 ₺ başlangıç + 300 ₺/ay, %6 aylık, 20 yıl.
Toplam yatırılan: 77.000 ₺. Nihai değer: ~155.500 ₺. Kazanılan faiz: ~78.500 ₺.

Örnek 3 — Erken başlamak ve geç başlamak:
200 ₺/ay, %7, aylık bileşik.
25 yaşında başla → 65 yaşında: nihai değer ≈ 525.000 ₺
35 yaşında başla → 65 yaşında: nihai değer ≈ 243.000 ₺
10 yıllık avantaj, 282.000 ₺ ek servet yaratır.
content.howItWorks_1: Başlangıç yatırımınızı (anapara), yıllık faiz oranını ve yıl cinsinden yatırım süresini girin. Bileşikleme sıklığını seçin — aylık, tasarruf hesapları ve yatırım platformları için en yaygın olanıdır. İsteğe bağlı olarak düzenli mevduatları modellemek için aylık katkı tutarı ekleyin.
content.howItWorks_2: Hesapla'ya tıklayın. Bileşik faiz formülü, seçtiğiniz sıklık için çalışır. Aylık katkılar girilmişse, anüite gelecek değeri formülü hesaplanır ve eklenir. Her iki bileşen toplam tahmini bakiyenizde birleşir.
content.howItWorks_3: Sonuçlarınızı inceleyin: Nihai Tutar, tahmini toplam bakiyedir. Toplam Katkılar, yatırdığınız gerçek para miktarıdır (anapara + tüm aylık ödemeler). Toplam Kazanılan Faiz, bileşiklemin yarattığı şeydir — ikisi arasındaki farktır. Özellikle uzun vadeli ufuklarda katkılar ile nihai tutar arasındaki büyük fark, bileşik faizin iş başında olduğunu gösterir.
content.whyItMatters:
Compound interest is the mathematical foundation of nearly every long-term financial goal — from retirement security to generational wealth. The single most important factor in maximizing compound growth is time. Waiting just 10 years to start investing can cost more than half of your final retirement savings at equivalent contribution levels, a fact that financial educators call the most underappreciated truth in personal finance.

The numbers make this concrete: a 25-year-old investing $300/month at 7% average annual return accumulates approximately $810,000 by age 65. A 35-year-old making identical investments accumulates approximately $362,000 — less than half, for only 10 fewer years. The missing $448,000 was not lost to bad decisions. It was lost to time. Every year of delay costs you a year of exponential compounding that cannot be recovered by later investing more.

Beyond retirement, compound interest powers 529 college savings plans, tax-deferred 401k and IRA accounts, emergency funds in high-yield savings, and index fund portfolios. Understanding how compounding works — and using this calculator to visualize your specific financial future — is among the highest-value activities available to any person at any income level. Even $50 per month invested consistently at age 22 grows to over $200,000 by age 65.

Compound interest also works against you when it comes to debt. Credit card debt at 20% APR compounding monthly means that an unpaid $5,000 balance grows to $8,954 in just 5 years without additional charges — more than $3,900 created by compounding alone. A $30,000 student loan at 6.5% over 20 years generates $24,000 in interest before it is paid off. Understanding compound interest from both sides — as a tool for wealth creation and a mechanism of debt growth — is essential for any rational personal financial plan. The same mathematical engine that builds retirement savings destroys financial stability when applied to high-interest debt.
content.limitations:
This calculator assumes a constant, fixed interest rate throughout the entire investment period. In practice, savings account rates fluctuate, bond yields change, and stock market returns vary dramatically year to year. Historical average returns (like the ~7% real return of the S&P 500) represent 50+ year trends, not annual guarantees. In any given year, actual returns can range from −40% to +50%. Sequence-of-returns risk — experiencing large losses early in a withdrawal phase — is not modeled here and can significantly affect real-world retirement outcomes.

The calculator does not account for inflation, taxes, or investment fees. Inflation of 2–3% per year steadily erodes the real value of your future balance. Investment fees — fund expense ratios, advisor fees — reduce effective returns. A 1% annual fee on a $100,000 portfolio compounding at 7% over 30 years costs approximately $174,000 in lost final value compared to a 0.05% fee fund. This is why low-cost index funds are so widely recommended: the savings in fees compound just as powerfully as returns.

For tax-advantaged accounts (401k, IRA, Roth IRA), after-tax outcomes depend on your marginal tax bracket at the time of withdrawal, which may differ significantly from your current bracket. Consult a certified financial planner for projections that account for your specific tax situation, asset allocation, and planned retirement date.
content.tips_0: Use this calculator to find your 'crossover point' — the year when your investment growth in a single year exceeds your annual contribution. At 7% on a $200/month contribution, the crossover happens around year 25, when the portfolio's annual growth surpasses $2,400 in new deposits. After the crossover, compounding does more work than you do. This visualization is the most powerful argument for starting early: the earlier you start, the sooner compounding becomes your primary wealth builder rather than your monthly deposits.
content.tips_1: Start investing now — even $50 or $100 per month matters. Time is the irreplaceable variable in the compound interest formula. At 7% annual return, $100/month starting at age 22 reaches approximately $433,000 by age 65. Starting at age 32 with the same amount reaches only $216,000. The decision to start is more valuable than any specific investment choice.
content.tips_2: Minimize fees relentlessly. A 1% annual expense ratio versus 0.05% costs you roughly $174,000 over 30 years on a $100,000 portfolio growing at 7%. Choose low-cost index ETFs (expense ratios under 0.20%) over actively managed funds. SPIVA research consistently shows 80–90% of active funds underperform their benchmark index over 15-year periods after fees.
content.tips_3: Maximize tax-advantaged accounts before taxable brokerage accounts. Roth IRA contributions grow and are withdrawn completely tax-free — the equivalent of 1–3% additional annual return depending on your tax bracket. Always contribute enough to your 401k to capture any employer match first — that match is an immediate 50–100% return on your contribution. In 2024, the 401k contribution limit is $23,000 ($30,500 for age 50+) and the IRA limit is $7,000 ($8,000 for age 50+). Maxing both gives you $30,000/year in tax-advantaged compounding — far more powerful than the same contributions in a taxable account.
content.tips_4: Automate contributions and ignore short-term market volatility. Set up automatic monthly transfers so investing happens without a decision each month. Investors who try to time the market consistently underperform those who invest consistently through market cycles. The most important behavior in compound interest investing is consistency over time — not timing. A well-documented behavioral finance finding: investors in mutual funds earn significantly less than the funds themselves return, because they buy after periods of strong performance and sell after downturns. Automating removes this self-sabotaging behavior and lets compounding do its work uninterrupted.
